summary:
The Abu Simbel temples, constructed during the reign of [[event:1807|Ramesses II]] between approximately 1264 and 1244 BC, stand as among the most impressive monuments of ancient Egyptian civilization. Carved directly into sandstone cliffs at a remote location in Nubia (modern southern Egypt near the Sudanese border), the complex comprises two temples: the Great Temple dedicated to Ramesses II himself and to the gods Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, and the smaller temple dedicated to his chief wife Nefertari and the goddess Hathor.

The Great Temple's facade features four colossal seated statues of Ramesses II, each approximately twenty meters tall. The interior is oriented so that twice a year — on approximately February 22 and October 22, dates corresponding roughly to the king's birthday and coronation — the rising sun illuminates the innermost sanctuary, casting light on the statues of the three gods and the pharaoh. Only the statue of Ptah, god of darkness, remains in shadow. This solar alignment demonstrates extraordinary astronomical and architectural skill.

Abu Simbel was built partly as a statement of Egyptian power in Nubia and partly as a self-deifying project, as Ramesses II had himself depicted as a god alongside the traditional deities. The temples were forgotten and buried under sand for centuries until rediscovered by the Swiss explorer Johann Ludwig Burckhardt in 1813. In the 1960s, when the construction of the Aswan High Dam threatened to submerge Abu Simbel under Lake Nasser, an extraordinary international engineering project funded by UNESCO cut the temples into sections and relocated them to an artificial hill 65 meters higher and 200 meters back from their original position, preserving them for posterity.
